section.title: I Love the Lord, for He Heard Me
section.sectionRationale: A deeply personal thanksgiving within the Passover Hallel, this psalm moves from the ropes of death to the cup of salvation, almost certainly among the hymns Jesus sang with His disciples after the Last Supper on His way toward His own death.
section.thesis: A love for the LORD born from being personally heard in the depths of death and distress overflows into public vows, a lifted cup of salvation, and thanksgiving offered in the presence of all God's people.
section.frameNote: Six movements: love born of answered prayer, verses 1-2; the ropes of death and the desperate cry, verses 3-4; rest in a gracious and compassionate God, verses 5-7; a walk in the land of the living, verses 8-11; the cup of salvation and vows fulfilled, verses 12-14; and precious saints and public praise in Jerusalem's courts, verses 15-19.

units.label: I love the LORD, for He heard me

verses.text: I love the LORD, for He has heard my voice, my appeal for mercy.
Because He has inclined His ear to me, I will call on Him as long as I live.

units.label: The ropes of death entangled me

verses.text: The ropes of death entangled me; the anguish of Sheol overcame me; I was confronted by trouble and sorrow.
Then I called on the name of the LORD: "O LORD, deliver my soul!"

units.label: Return to your rest, O my soul

verses.text: The LORD is gracious and righteous; our God is full of compassion.
The LORD preserves the {{peti|simplehearted}}; I was helpless, and He saved me.
Return to your rest, O my soul, for the LORD has been good to you.

units.label: I will walk before the LORD
units.note: in the land of the living

verses.text: For You have delivered my soul from death, my eyes from tears, my feet from stumbling.
I will walk before the LORD in the land of the living.
I believed, therefore I said, "I am greatly afflicted."
In my alarm I said, "All men are liars!"

units.label: I will lift the cup of salvation

verses.text: How can I repay the LORD for all His goodness to me?
I will lift the {{kos_yeshuah|cup of salvation}} and call on the name of the LORD.
I will fulfill my vows to the LORD in the presence of all His people.

units.label: Precious in the sight of the LORD
units.note: the death of His saints, public praise

verses.text: Precious in the sight of the LORD is the death of His saints.
Truly, O LORD, I am Your servant; I am Your servant, the son of Your maidservant; You have broken my bonds.
I will offer to You a sacrifice of thanksgiving and call on the name of the LORD.
I will fulfill my vows to the LORD in the presence of all His people,
in the courts of the LORD's house, in your midst, O Jerusalem. Hallelujah!

peti.gloss: Simplehearted, unpretentious, one who relies honestly on God rather than on cunning or self-sufficiency. God's preservation of such people, verse 6, shows His care reaches the humble and undefended, not merely the strong.

kos_yeshuah.gloss: The cup of salvation, likely a libation offered in thanksgiving for deliverance. This same cup imagery, lifted at Passover, is echoed by Jesus at the Last Supper when He takes the cup and calls it the new covenant in His blood, Luke 22:20.

basic.context: This deeply personal psalm of thanksgiving recounts being tangled in death's ropes and Sheol's anguish, crying out to the LORD, and being delivered, moving the psalmist to public vows, a lifted cup of salvation, and thanksgiving offered before the whole worshiping community.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm models an honest faith that does not hide affliction, even the psalmist's own moment of despair, all men are liars, verse 11, but channels every experience, distress and deliverance alike, into deepened love and public testimony rather than private, silent relief.
basic.originalLanguages: God preserves the peti, the simplehearted, verse 6, those who rely honestly on Him rather than on their own cunning. And the psalmist vows to lift the kos yeshuah, the cup of salvation, verse 13, sacred imagery that Jesus Himself takes up at the Last Supper when He identifies the cup with His own blood of the new covenant, Luke 22:20.
basic.typology: This psalm was almost certainly among the Hallel hymns Jesus sang with His disciples immediately after instituting the Lord's Supper, Matthew 26:30, meaning its cup of salvation, its ropes of death, and its declaration that the death of His saints is precious to the LORD were on Jesus' own lips just before He walked to Gethsemane and the cross.
basic.christ: Jesus likely sang this very psalm after the Last Supper, on His way to the garden and the cross, Matthew 26:30, and its cup of salvation directly anticipates the cup He blessed and identified with the new covenant in His blood, Luke 22:20. The psalm's confession that precious in the sight of the LORD is the death of His saints, verse 15, finds its deepest meaning in Christ's own death, through which He secured resurrection life for all who trust Him, and its picture of deliverance from death, verse 8, is fulfilled completely in His resurrection.
basic.god: God is gracious, righteous, and full of compassion, inclining His ear to hear even the simplehearted and helpless. He delivers His people from death itself and counts the death of His faithful ones as precious, never wasted or overlooked.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 26:30 records Jesus and His disciples singing a hymn, likely including this psalm, after the Last Supper. Luke 22:20 records Jesus taking the cup and naming it the new covenant in His blood, the fulfillment of this psalm's cup of salvation. 2 Corinthians 4:13 quotes verse 10, I believed, therefore I said, applying it to Paul's own gospel confidence amid suffering.
basic.oneStory: A soul entangled in death's ropes cries out and is delivered, moving to public thanksgiving and a lifted cup of salvation, a psalm Jesus Himself sang on His way to the cross, where He drank a far bitterer cup so that His people, redeemed from death, might walk before the LORD forever in the land of the living.

context.literary: A personal thanksgiving psalm moving through invocation, verses 1-2, crisis, verses 3-4, confession of God's character, verses 5-7, testimony of deliverance, verses 8-11, vows of gratitude, verses 12-14, and a closing declaration of the value of the saints' lives and public praise, verses 15-19, with the trouble-prayer-rescue rhythm repeating throughout.
context.historical: Its lack of a named author allows any worshiper, whether David in a season of crisis, or a returning exile, to sing it as personal testimony, and as one of the Egyptian Hallel psalms, 113-118, it was recited at Passover, likely among the very hymns Jesus and His disciples sang after their final Passover meal together.
context.canonical: Standing at the heart of the Hallel between the corporate praise of Psalm 115 and the brief universal call of Psalm 117, this psalm personalizes the collection's themes of deliverance, moving from Israel's story to one worshiper's own story of rescue from death.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model a faith that responds to answered prayer not with private relief alone but with a lifelong resolve to keep calling on God, public vows fulfilled before the whole community, and open testimony to what the LORD has done.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes one worshiper's specific deliverance from mortal danger; it prescribes the abiding pattern of honest lament, grateful public testimony, and the fulfillment of vows made in distress, none of which are private matters to be quietly forgotten once the crisis passes.

debates.question: How should verse 15, precious in the sight of the LORD is the death of His saints, be understood alongside the psalm's overall theme of deliverance from death?

views.label: The verse means God does not lightly permit the death of His faithful ones; it happens only within His sovereign, watchful care
views.case: The immediate context celebrates deliverance from death, verse 8, so verse 15 most naturally continues that theme, God does not cheaply or carelessly allow His covenant people to die, and when death does come to them, it is not outside His attentive care.

views.label: The verse specifically values the death itself as precious, a costly and significant event in God's sight, not merely a rare occurrence He reluctantly permits
views.case: The Hebrew term for precious, yaqar, elsewhere denotes something of great worth or value in itself, suggesting the verse teaches that the death of God's saints, however painful, is meaningful and valuable to Him, not merely something He seeks to prevent, a truth the New Testament deepens in passages like Revelation 14:13.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that no believer's death occurs outside God's watchful, purposeful care, and that whatever a saint's death costs in human grief, it is never overlooked or wasted in God's sight.

study.originalLanguages: God preserves the peti, the simplehearted, verse 6, those who rely honestly on Him rather than their own cunning or defenses. The psalmist vows to lift the kos yeshuah, the cup of salvation, verse 13, temple and Passover imagery that Jesus Himself takes up at the Last Supper, identifying the cup with the new covenant in His own blood, Luke 22:20.

typology.type: The cup of salvation lifted in thanksgiving, verse 13
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's cup at the Last Supper, the new covenant in His blood, poured out for many, Luke 22:20.

typology.type: Deliverance of the soul from death, verse 8
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled completely in Christ's own resurrection from the dead, guaranteeing eternal life to all who trust Him, 2 Timothy 1:10.

study.christ: This psalm was very likely among the Hallel hymns Jesus sang with His disciples immediately after the Last Supper, Matthew 26:30, meaning He walked toward Gethsemane and the cross with its very words on His lips. Its cup of salvation, verse 13, anticipates the cup Jesus took and identified as the new covenant in His blood, Luke 22:20, and its confession that the death of His saints is precious to the LORD, verse 15, finds its fullest meaning in Christ's own death, through which He secured for His people the resurrection life this psalm's deliverance from death, verse 8, ultimately points toward.
study.god: God is gracious, righteous, and full of compassion, attentive even to the simplehearted and helpless who cry out to Him. He delivers His people from death itself, and He regards the death of His faithful ones as precious, never careless or wasted in His sight.

crossReferences.note: Records Jesus and His disciples singing a hymn, almost certainly including this psalm, immediately after the Last Supper.
Jesus takes the cup and names it the new covenant in His blood, the fulfillment of this psalm's cup of salvation.
Paul quotes verse 10, I believed, therefore I said, applying this psalm's confidence amid suffering to his own gospel ministry.

study.oneStory: A soul entangled in death's ropes cries out and is delivered, responding with public vows and a lifted cup of salvation, the very psalm Jesus sang on His way to the cross, where He drank a bitterer cup than any worshiper here imagined, so that His people, delivered from death forever, might walk before the LORD in the land of the living without end.

quiz.q: Why does the psalmist say in verse 1 that he loves the LORD?

options.text: Because the LORD has heard his voice, his appeal for mercy.
options.note: Love here is grounded in the concrete experience of being heard, not an abstract feeling.

options.text: Because the LORD has given him great wealth.
options.note: The verse specifically credits being heard in prayer, not material wealth, as the reason for love.

options.text: Because the LORD has removed all future trouble from his life.
options.note: The psalm describes past deliverance from real trouble, not a promise of a trouble-free future.

options.text: Because the LORD publicly honored him before kings.
options.note: The reason given is that God heard his personal appeal for mercy, not public honor before rulers.

quiz.q: How does the psalmist describe his crisis in verse 3?

options.text: The ropes of death entangled him and the anguish of Sheol overcame him.
options.note: This vivid imagery of being bound by death sets up the desperate cry that follows in verse 4.

options.text: He faced only mild inconvenience and minor annoyance.
options.note: The description is of severe, life-threatening distress, not minor inconvenience.

options.text: He suffered from an abundance of wealth and comfort.
options.note: The crisis described is one of danger and anguish, the opposite of abundance and comfort.

options.text: He experienced a brief moment of doubt about God's existence.
options.note: The crisis is described in terms of mortal danger, not merely intellectual doubt.

quiz.q: What vow does the psalmist make in verse 13 regarding the cup of salvation?

options.text: He will lift the cup of salvation and call on the name of the LORD.
options.note: This cup imagery is later taken up by Jesus at the Last Supper as the new covenant in His blood.

options.text: He will pour out the cup as a curse on his enemies.
options.note: The cup here is lifted in thanksgiving and calling on God's name, not poured out as a curse.

options.text: He will refuse to drink from any cup again.
options.note: The vow is to lift the cup in grateful worship, not to abstain from it.

options.text: He will share the cup only with strangers, never with God's people.
options.note: The following verses describe fulfilling vows in the presence of all His people, not strangers.

quiz.q: How does verse 15 describe the death of God's saints?

options.text: Precious in the sight of the LORD.
options.note: This assures that the death of God's faithful ones is never careless or overlooked in His sight.

options.text: Meaningless and forgotten by God.
options.note: The verse says the opposite; their death is precious, not meaningless, to the LORD.

options.text: A punishment for hidden sin.
options.note: The verse frames their death as precious and valued, not as a specifically punitive act.

options.text: An event God actively causes without regard for His people.
options.note: The verse emphasizes God's precious regard for His saints' death, not careless causation.

quiz.q: How does Luke 22:20 connect to this psalm's cup of salvation?

options.text: Jesus takes the cup at the Last Supper and identifies it as the new covenant in His blood.
options.note: This directly fulfills and deepens the psalm's cup of salvation imagery, likely sung immediately afterward as part of the Hallel.

options.text: It has no connection to this psalm's cup imagery.
options.note: The connection is strong; Jesus' words at the Last Supper directly echo and fulfill this psalm's cup of salvation.

options.text: It describes a completely different, unrelated cup of judgment.
options.note: The cup in Luke 22:20 is one of covenant and salvation, closely paralleling this psalm's cup of salvation, not judgment.

options.text: It refers only to a future millennial banquet with no present significance.
options.note: Luke 22:20 refers to Christ's immediate, historical institution of the Lord's Supper, not solely a future banquet.

questions: The psalmist loves God specifically because God heard his cry for mercy in real distress. Can you name a specific time God heard you, and has that shaped your love for Him the way it shaped the psalmist's?

The psalmist is honest enough to admit, in my alarm I said, all men are liars. Where do you need to bring an honest, even messy, emotion to God rather than presenting Him a tidier version of your faith?

This psalm was very likely sung by Jesus on His way to Gethsemane and the cross. How does it change your reading of His last hours to imagine Him singing of the ropes of death and the cup of salvation just before He faced both?

The psalmist vows to fulfill his promises to God in the presence of all His people, not privately. What vow or commitment to God do you need to make more publicly accountable, rather than keeping it merely private?
God counts the death of His saints as precious, never careless or wasted. How does this truth comfort you regarding the death of a believer you love, or your own mortality?

translationNotes.note: The Berean Standard Bible follows the Hebrew, I believed, therefore I said; the ancient Greek Septuagint reads, therefore I have spoken, the wording Paul quotes in 2 Corinthians 4:13.
The cup of salvation likely refers to a libation cup lifted in a thanksgiving offering, temple and Passover imagery that Jesus draws on directly at the Last Supper, Luke 22:20.
Precious renders yaqar, denoting something rare and costly, teaching that the death of God's faithful ones is never careless or overlooked in His sight.
